# Results

## Transmembrane Potential

The figure below shows the transmembrane potential $V_m$ at mid-activation.
In the HTML version this is a fully interactive 3D viewer — you can rotate,
zoom, and inspect the mesh. In the PDF version it is a high-resolution static
render from the camera angle you locked in the dashboard.

{{< 4d-image src="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam" field="Vm" fields="Vm,activationTime,activationVelocity" id="fig-vm" style="vm-dark" caption="Transmembrane potential $V_m$ at mid-activation" >}}

## Activation Time

{{< 4d-image src="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam" field="activationTime" id="fig-at" time="last" caption="Activation time distribution" >}}

## Transmembrane Potential Animation

In the HTML output this is a fully interactive scrubber. In the PDF, videos cannot be embedded — use
`{{< 4d-timeseries >}}` instead (see @sec-timeseries) for a static multi-frame strip.

{{< 4d-video src="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam" field="Vm" fps="10" id="vid-vm" caption="Transmembrane potential $V_m$ over time (HTML only)" >}}

## Activation Time-Series (HTML + PDF) {#sec-timeseries}

The timeseries shortcode generates N equally-spaced snapshots from the simulation. In the HTML output
each snapshot is a fully interactive 3D viewer sharing a single synchronised camera (rotating one
panel rotates all). In the PDF export a composite PNG strip is rendered from the locked camera angle.

Use the 🔓 padlock button on any panel to lock the camera before exporting.

{{< 4d-timeseries src="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam" field="Vm" id="ts-vm" steps="4" height="300px" caption="$V_m$ at four equally-spaced time steps — synced camera, HTML and PDF compatible" >}}

## Side-by-Side Comparison (independent cameras)

Each panel retains its own independent camera. The 🔓 padlock button in each viewer lets you lock its
camera angle before running Export PDF.

{{< 4d-panel id="panel-compare" layout="2x1" height="500px"
    caption="$V_m$ (left) vs activation time (right) — independent cameras."
    src1="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam"
    id1="fig-vm" field1="Vm" fields1="Vm,activationTime,activationVelocity"
    src2="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam"
    id2="fig-at" field2="activationTime" fields2="Vm,activationTime,activationVelocity" >}}

## Side-by-Side Comparison (synchronised camera)

With `camera="sync"` all subfigures share one camera file. Rotating any panel rotates all; the camera
ack badge appears on every viewer simultaneously.

{{< 4d-panel id="panel-sync" layout="2x1" height="500px" camera="sync"
    caption="$V_m$ (left) vs activation time (right) — synchronised camera."
    src1="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam"
    id1="fig-vm-sync" field1="Vm"
    src2="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam"
    id2="fig-at-sync" field2="activationTime" >}}

## ParaView Pipeline Figure

`{{< 4d-pvsm >}}` renders a full ParaView state file (`.pvsm`) rather than a raw OpenFOAM case.
`pvpython` executes the pipeline, writes an intermediate `.vtu`, and PyVista then exports an
interactive vtk.js viewer. This lets you embed any ParaView filter chain — clipping planes,
streamlines, isovolumes — directly in the paper.

{{< 4d-pvsm src="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/example_state.pvsm" data="/Users/simaocastro/cardiacFoamEP/tutorials/NiedererEtAl2012/Niederer.foam" id="fig-pvsm-vm" caption="Transmembrane voltage rendered via ParaView pipeline" >}}

## Interactive 2D/3D Data Graphs (Plotly JSON)

You can now easily embed high-quality declarative data visualization charts using Plotly's standard `.json` export feature (`fig.write_json()`).

These interactive JavaScript-powered graphs handle their own zooming, panning, and toggling tools, taking the computational load off your HTML bundle. More importantly, when building a static PDF, these are automatically rasterized via the Kaleido engine into ultra-high-resolution images keeping the compilation process robust and decoupled.

{{< 4d-graph src="media/example_graph.json" id="fig-plotly-surface" caption="Example mathematical 3D surface seamlessly exported as a Plotly JSON graph." >}}

## Traditional Static Figures (PNG/JPG)

You absolutely don't need a `4d-` shortcode for normal static screenshots or diagram files like PNGs or JPGs. Because 4Dpaper is essentially a customized Quarto framework under the hood, you can just use standard Markdown image syntax!

It will reliably scale appropriately in both the dashboard HTML preview and the compiled exported PDF.

![A classic Matplotlib graph embedded as a standard PNG image.](media/static_example.png){#fig-static-example width="80%"}
